name: Approach
intuition_and_algorithm:
We want to count all square submatrices in a binary matrix that are filled with 1s.
                            Instead of brute force checking each possible square (which is expensive), we use
                            dynamic programming to reuse results of smaller subproblems.
                            If we know the largest square ending at a certain cell, we can build larger squares from it.
                        

Create a DP table dp[n][m] where dp[i][j] represents
                                the size of the largest square submatrix with bottom-right corner at
                                (i, j).
                            
Initialization:
                                
First row and first column remain the same as the input matrix
                                        since no bigger square can be formed there.


Transition:
                                
If arr[i][j] == 0 â no square possible â dp[i][j] = 0.
                                    
If arr[i][j] == 1 â
                                        dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]).
                                        This ensures the current cell extends the smallest possible square from its
                                        neighbors.
                                    


Each dp[i][j] contributes its value to the total count,
                                since if a square of size k ends here, then it contains
                                k smaller squares within it.

complexities.time: O(n Ã m) â each cell is processed once.
complexities.space: O(n Ã m) â DP table used.

code.python:
class Solution:
    # Function to count total square submatrices with all 1s
    def countSquares(self, n, m, arr):
        # DP table to store size of largest square ending at (i,j)
        dp = [[0]*m for _ in range(n)]

        # Initialize first row
        for j in range(m):
            dp[0][j] = arr[0][j]

        # Initialize first column
        for i in range(n):
            dp[i][0] = arr[i][0]

        # Fill the rest of DP table
        for i in range(1, n):
            for j in range(1, m):
                # If current cell is 0, no square ends here
                if arr[i][j] == 0:
                    dp[i][j] = 0
                else:
                    # Minimum of top, left, top-left + 1
                    dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i][j-1])

        # Sum up all values in DP table
        total = sum(sum(row) for row in dp)

        # Return total count of squares
        return total

# Input matrix
arr = [
    [0, 1, 1, 1],
    [1, 1, 1, 1],
    [0, 1, 1, 1]
]

# Matrix dimensions
n, m = 3, 4

# Create Solution object
obj = Solution()

# Call function
squares = obj.countSquares(n, m, arr)

# Print the result
print("The number of squares:", squares)
